speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 4th, 1995. The survivor is Anna Goldenberg-Schwartcz. And this is tape number three. Please continue where you left off.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned, my mother was very much concerned about my education. Now, that young lady, Jana Finkiel, with her husband, Motke Finkiel, fled to Russia. They remained in Kiev. And he volunteered to go to the Russian army to fight the Germans. She remained behind. At that time, the Germans took the Russian-- the Kiev Jewry and exterminated them in Babi Yar. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was the one that was supposedly shot. And on top of her were other corpse. When things got quiet, she crept out of the corpse, naked, and left. As she was walking in the street in Kiev, she saw from far away a policeman. She went down a side street. And in the side street, she was afraid that he'll follow her. All of a sudden, she saw an outhouse, AND she jumped in that filth and-- to save her life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then she came out of there and went to a-- to a home. When it got quiet, knocked at the door and asked people to help her. They helped her get washed, and they gave her something to wear. And she came back to her husband parents, to our town. Now, my mother was financially in a better position than that family, so she didn't want to hand them down just to share a piece of bread, or whatever she had with them. So she had an idea that she would ask that young lady, Jana Finkiel, to tutor me, to-- to teach me, because she was a university student, and I was still in high school, so that I don't waste the time. And this way, she'll pay her. And it will be a dignified way how to give to help her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on one of the lessons, Jana told me that story. As terrible as it sounds, I registered in my mind that story. And I never forgot about it. And that came handy to me much later in my survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After a while in the ghetto, there were rumors that the extermination will probably not omit us, because the other neighboring little-- little towns, people ran away and they told us that the Germans came and exterminated them. And they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother tried again to send us away. She found a place for my brother. And in a-- after a few days, my brother came back. He could not adjust to it. We couldn't go together. If my brother and I would wound up in a-- in a strange place somewhere by-- by other people, they would say, where did these two children come? They would understand right away that we were Jews. We had to be separate.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother found a place for me in a Czech family. There were three other girls. And they said that I am a cousin. And I worked with them together, with the understanding that, if there will be a need where my mother will have to-- to flee the ghetto, that she can come to this place. And the Czech women promised that she'll make a shelter for us. And that's how it happened. I was there a few weeks. And one night, my mother and my brother came running. And they said that they were digging the ditches a few days ago. And that night, the Germans came and loaded truckloads with all the Jewish inhabitants from the ghetto, and they took them there, and they shot them. My mother and my brother were lucky to be able to escape. And they came to us. The whole territory was proclaimed Judenrein. It means that they are clear of Jews. No Jew was supposed to live there. If there was a Jew, he was killed right away. The-- the Ukrainian police were very helpful with them. If-- if the Germans didn't find anybody, they did. And they-- either they killed them on the spot, or they brought them to the police station. That woman, the name is Doleczek.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-O-L-E-C-Z-E-K. Doleczek. Had a shelter in the middle of the field. That was already after the harvest. It was October time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a big hole in the ground, in the middle of the field. We walked-- we came in there. And on top of it, we were covered-- whatever was left from the harvest. So if Ukranians, or whoever passed by, they couldn't see that something was going on. 10 people were hidden there-- my mother, and my brother, and I, and a cousin of mine-- Malka was her name. And the other were from our city too.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there for six weeks. It was a small hole. We couldn't sit. We were standing. There was nothing to eat, obviously. We couldn't do there a thing. We just sat there, day by day. In the evening, we came out, when it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you sat? Or you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We stood. There was nothing to sit. We just-- we leaned, one against the other. In the evening, we went out. We relieved ourselves. And we went to beg some food. That woman, Doleczek, couldn't provide for all of us, so we went to the nearby places and asked for-- for a mere piece of bread, so that it can sustain us-- bread and water. And that's how it-- I had very thick braids. I did not unbraid and comb my hair for the entire six weeks, till one day in November, when we came out in the evening. There was a early snow. It was premature. Because usually, a snow is in December. We, the 10 people, understood that we cannot go back, because the footsteps are tracing and finishing right where the hole is. So we decided that we must go somewhere else. This place is finished for us. That woman, Doleczek, said that she has a little bunker near her house that-- for the young girls. We were four young girls. My cousin, Malka, I, and two other young girls can stay there. My mother said that she will go with my brother somewhere. And when she'll find a place, she'll come to pick me up. And that's how I understood what will be. The other side don't even know, because we were very upset. We didn't know what to do with ourselves. We went into that little bunker. It was close to the house, so that the-- the snow steps were not visible enough. In the morning, we heard a knock at that bunker. And when we looked up, there were Ukrainian police. With the rifles in their hands, they told us to come out. We came out. They brought us to the house. Now, the chief of the police was a young man. All the policemen that were Ukrainian were very young, but they were bloodthirsty. They were anti-Semites. That one chief of the police, his name was Mit'ka Kharchyshyn. He was a schoolmate of ours. And he favored, very much, my cousin, Malka. She was a very pretty girl. And I was sure that he will save her. I said to her, Malka, you know what you-- please help me and do something for me. So she said, you don't have to ask me, you know that I would do my best. And for a moment, I reminded myself the story about Jana Finkiel and what she told me when she left Babi Yar and emerged in that outhouse. And I asked them if I can go out to relieve myself. He said, yes. And I went out. And over there, where the outhouse was, I was ready to do what Jana did. And then I looked around. And near it, was another door. And was a little room with a bed where the farmhand slept. It was his quarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So rather than to jump in that filth, I went into that, opened the door, and went in there, and tried to push myself under the bed. I was very thin. And I was able to push myself under the bed. And with straw, covered myself all around and lied flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside, I heard-- I couldn't see, because I was inside-- but I heard outside a big commotion-- screaming and shooting. And that went on for a long time, for a long time. I was lying motionless, not knowing what's going on. But I was sure that my mother and my brother went away, because that's what my mother told me, that she is going away to look for another place. In the evening, that farmhand came and lied down on the bed. And I was under that bed, lying flat, afraid to breathe even. Then I heard, again, a commotion. The police came and were looking for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I heard them say in Ukrainian, where is that [NON-ENGLISH] girl? She disappeared under our hands. And they asked him, don't you really know where she is? He said, no. I don't know. He really didn't know. Because if he would have known that I'm right under the bed, I don't know if he would have let me be there. He probably would have told the police where I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, the following day, they came again. And they were searching again. And I heard them again say, it is incredible how that girl just-- just disappeared from under our hands. I stayed there under that bed for a few days and nights, and fantasizing what am I going to tell my mother how I escaped. And every time I thought about it, I had-- like somebody gives a transfusion-- full of life. And I thought to myself, my mother will be so proud how I was able to, in such circumstances, to help myself. And that kept me alive, flat on my back, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't leave. I assume I was there four days and four nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Urinated there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But then, I felt very weak. And I thought to myself, either I have to get out of there, or I'll die. And I decided to get out. In the morning, early in the morning, at dawn, the farmhand left the bed and went to his chores. I sneaked out. And when-- it was a big farm. And it was after the harvest. And it was by a-- the houses were stuck up with straw. So where the-- where the stack with straw was, I crept in there to-- to think what I have to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I-- when I breathe the fresh air and I looked up in the sky, I thought to myself, it's still a world. Still a world. What am I supposed to do now? And then I decided, I'm going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned before, I was very sheltered and overprotected. I-- that road was not familiar to me at all. I didn't know where to go. But somehow, I was pushed in a direction. And I went, maybe a kilometer or two, and then I saw a house on the hill. I went up there and knocked at the door. I didn't know who-- who that-- whose house it is and who will answer. It was very dangerous, because Jews were not allowed to live anymore. And if it would be a Ukrainian house, he would take me, bring me right away to the police, or would shot me on the-- on the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I knocked at the door, a Czech woman opened the door. And she knew me. She used to deal with my parents. She even used to bring butter to sell my mother. She was just startled to see me. Took me into the house, and gave me a little bit to-- to eat, just a little bit. And I asked her, do you know where my mother is? She said, no. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me what happened to me, and I told her. And I said to her, please, give me a pair of scissors and cut me off my braids. It was itching. It was full of lice. For-- for maybe six, seven weeks, I did not comb it. I had very thick braids. She said, I don't have any scissors. But come, I'll help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: She put a white sheet on the floor, and put gasoline in my hair, and combed out my lice, again, and again, and again. Every time, she gave me a little bit more to eat, a little bit. And then she said, you cannot stay here. You know what? You have to go where it's-- in another little room where there's no-- because if neighbors will come, I cannot tell them that-- that you're here. It's dangerous for me too. And that other little room when-- where I came in, I saw a dress of my friend. And I said to her, is my friend, Chana Kulisz, here? I would like to see her. So she thought for a minute, and she said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out later that my friend and her family, her parents, were hidden there. And the Czech woman-- their name is Kucer, the Czech family. There was a mother and a brother. And Mania was the one-- the one that combed my hair-- begged the Jewish family they should take me into their bunker, into their shelter. And then, there's the son said, I'll give her the bread that she eats. She's a little girl. I knew her parents. I don't want her to get lost. So my friend's father, Jakob Kulisz, said, I am determined to save my family. That's all I care. He didn't want me to get there. I didn't know it. I found out much later. After I was there a few days, the Kucers told me, you cannot stay here. I'm sorry. I had nowhere to go. But if I couldn't stay there, I went further.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a bridge where Hungarian soldiers were guarding. So that Venush Kucer, the son, said, you know what? I'll take you with my horse and buggy over the bridge and bring you wherever you want to go. I said, I want to go to the other village where I-- where I was before on the pasture with the cows. I thought, maybe I'll be able to stay there. Took me there. During the day, I was hidden in the-- in the straw stacks. And at night, I knocked at the door and came. [INAUDIBLE] When that woman-- her name was, Mahowa, was her name, a Czech woman-- when she saw me, she almost fainted. And she said, your father sent grain and fed all of Europe. And you come to us for a piece of bread? It's the end of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not sentimental about it. I was just waiting for her to give me that piece of bread. She gave me some food. And my-- I had very big wounds on my legs, so she gave me some salve, some remedies, homemade remedies, a leaf she put on, and a piece of rag. She bandaged it. And she told me, you cannot stay here. I'm sorry. If people will find out, then they will kill me, and they will kill you. Now, in that village, it was near our shtetl. Chomut is the name. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-O-M-U-T. It's a Czech village with very prosperous Czech people. And my father dealt with every one of them. And they were very favorable for my father. So I thought to myself, I'm going to knock on everybody's door and do what I can do. That's what I did for a few weeks. It was a-- that winter, in 1942, was a very, very severe winter. I had no clothes, nothing. Whatever I had on me, that's what I was wearing, day in and day out. During the day, I was hidden in that haystack. And the mice were above my head, and all kinds of creatures. But I tolerated everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was so strong that I'm thinking, now, where does a young girl that was so overprotected take so much strength to endure everything? I was cut off from the whole world. I didn't know whether my mother lived, and my brother. I fantasized that she is. I didn't want to believe that she's not there.

speaker: interviewee
text: (TEARFUL) And-- and no trace of anybody. Didn't know whether Jews are still existing somewhere. Nothing. Nothing, just myself with that strong willpower to survive and push from one minute to the other, from one day to the other. Now, after a few weeks, I was in a-- in a house where they were the most friendly to my father. And at that house, they were the most prosperous Czechs. Their name was Husak-- H-U-S-A-K. When the Germans came, there was the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] means the agriculture committee. When they came, they used to party there. It was a Sunday-- probably a Sunday evening. When I knocked at the door, she came in and she said, wait. I have, in my living room, the Germans. Please. She said, go to the stall where there are the horses, and I'll bring you some food. She came in. She brought me some food. And she said, you cannot stay here, because if they see you, they'll kill you, and they'll kill me. And rumors are that you are in this village. I heard about it. And I left the place-- the snow was almost over my head-- and walked. It were destiny that I did not know, but somehow my intuition was leading me, was carrying me to the same road that-- that I went before, that the Kucer told [INAUDIBLE]. At that time, the Hungarian police was not already on the-- on the road. And somehow, I came back to that Kucer's home. When they-- when I came in there, they heard about me. They heard that I'm making this-- the circle. They gave me some food. And in the evening, their sister came. And she said to me-- her name is Antonina Shindelazova. She lived across the street. She had a little farm just in the same village, [NON-ENGLISH], but on the other side of the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, would you like to come with me and to see some Jews? I did not believe what I hear. Such an offer. I don't think I can equate it with anything that somebody would tell me now, what I would like to. It was like giving somebody life, when he has a death sentence. This is how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, in the evening, my niece will bring me to you. At night, when it was dark-- the niece was Frantiska-- she brought me to her farm. And I came in to her-- it was a tiny little house-- I opened up the door. There was the Goldenberg family. It was Szloma, and the mother, Rachel Goldenberg, the daughter, Sonja, with her son, George, and a brother, Froiko, with his wife, Asia, and the niece, Tania. There were seven people from that family. They heard what happened to me. And they knew that my mother and my brother were killed (TEARFUL) on the premises by Doleczek that day that I was caught. That my mother never left, that she was there. And they asked Antonina Shindelazova, the Czech woman, to bring me to her house and be in the shelter with them.
